next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

By four o'clock Lena had already changed the sheets, shaved both legs past the point of common sense, and burned a narrow stripe of skin above her lip with a wax strip that promised salon results and delivered a hot paper tear. The date was not until seven-thirty. Eli was meeting her at a wine bar off Passyunk with mismatched stools and good fries, and she had told herself three different times that she was not acting like a woman about to be inspected by God.

The apartment kept the late-summer heat the way a grudge kept shape. Her box fan shoved the smell of hot dust and old headphone foam from the desk to the bed and back again. Below her, in the tailor shop, somebody dropped a metal hanger and swore in Spanish. Out on Moore Street a scooter coughed past, then another. Every sound came up through the floorboards thin and cheap and neighborly. It made the place feel more real, which should have helped. Instead it made her feel like the whole block was watching her put on mascara.

She tried to work until five and got through eleven minutes of a caption file before realizing she had typed the same speaker identification twice and missed an entire sentence because Eli had sent a photo of his hand wrapped around a coffee cup with the caption leaving load-out in an hour, don't let me be underdressed next to you. It was the kind of line he was good at, a light touch over a live wire. He could flirt without sounding like he had set out to flirt. He could make attention feel like weather, like something she had not caused but had somehow earned.

She texted back, `T...
open_hooks_to_carry: Lena's divorce-era insecurity about her body is concrete, not abstract.
Eli's erotic attention feels observant enough to bypass her defenses.
prev_tail_excerpt:
"And yet I'm right."

Lena smiled despite herself. "Maybe."

"Maybe nothing. Go upstairs. Text me when Lopez only has corpse cilantro so I can blame Mateo."

"You are sending a sixteen-year-old out for herbs at nine-thirty."

"I bought him sneakers. That's called governance."

Lena snorted. June started back toward the corner, smoke trailing behind her in the damp air. Halfway down the block she lifted a hand without turning around.

Upstairs, Lena let herself into the apartment and stood for a minute in the stale heat, leftovers in her arms, listening to the room take her weight. The place felt smaller after Gabe's house, as it always did, more deliberate in its loneliness. She set the containers on the table, kicked off her shoes, and checked her phone.

There was a text from Gabe already.

`okay fine. podcast can come around`

And under it, from Eli:

`You sounded happy.`

Then:

`I liked hearing you in there.`

Lena sat on the edge of her bed without meaning to and read both messages twice. Sauce and fennel and other people's detergent still clung to her shirt. Outside, a bus sighed at the corner and somebody laughed loud enough to bounce off the brick opposite her window. She typed back to Eli, deleted it, typed again.

For once the apartment did not feel like the only place where he existed.
state_deltas_required: Lena's body shame and Eli's apparently patient attention
The relationship acquires physical consequence when Lena lets Eli see and direct her body on camera.
